name: llm-app-patterns
description: Production-ready patterns for building LLM applications. Covers RAG pipelines, agent architectures, prompt IDEs, and LLMOps monitoring. Use when designing AI applications, implementing RAG, building agents, or setting up LLM observability.
capabilities: Design RAG pipelines with chunking, embedding, and retrieval strategies
Implement agent architectures including ReAct, function calling, and multi-agent systems
Create prompt templates with versioning and A/B testing
Set up LLMOps monitoring for latency, quality, and cost metrics
Apply production patterns like caching, rate limiting, and fallbacks
author: sickn33
securitySummary: This skill is a documentation file containing educational content about LLM application patterns. All static analysis findings are false positives caused by markdown formatting. The backticks flagged are code block delimiters and ASCII art borders, not shell command execution. URLs are documentation references, not active network calls. Code examples like hashlib.sha256 are illustrative and use secure algorithms. No executable code or security risks detected.
valueStatement: Building LLM applications requires navigating complex architectural decisions. This skill provides battle-tested patterns for RAG pipelines, agent systems, and production operations.
limitations: Code examples are illustrative and require adaptation to specific frameworks
Does not provide ready-to-use library implementations
Vector database choices require external setup and configuration
LLM API integration requires valid API keys and accounts

useCases.targetUser: Backend developers building AI-powered support tools
useCases.title: RAG Knowledge Base
useCases.description: Build a question-answering system grounded in your documentation using hybrid search and contextual compression.

useCases.targetUser: AI engineers developing autonomous workflow automation
useCases.title: Agent Task Automation
useCases.description: Create multi-step agents that can search, calculate, and synthesize information using the ReAct or Plan-and-Execute patterns.

useCases.targetUser: ML Ops engineers managing production AI systems
useCases.title: LLM Production Monitoring
useCases.description: Implement observability for LLM applications with metrics tracking, distributed tracing, and evaluation frameworks.

promptTemplates.title: Basic RAG Prompt
promptTemplates.scenario: Use for simple retrieval-augmented generation when you need to ground responses in provided documents

promptTemplates.title: ReAct Agent Prompt
promptTemplates.prompt:
You are an AI assistant that can use tools to answer questions.

Available tools:
{tools_description}

Use this format:
Thought: [your reasoning about what to do next]
Action: [tool_name(arguments)]
Observation: [tool result]
... (repeat as needed)
Thought: I have enough information to answer
Final Answer: [your response]

Question: {question}
promptTemplates.scenario: Use when building agents that need to reason through multi-step problems using external tools

promptTemplates.title: Few-Shot Prompt with Examples
promptTemplates.scenario: Use when you need consistent output format or behavior by demonstrating examples

promptTemplates.title: Prompt Chain for Research
promptTemplates.scenario: Use for complex tasks requiring multiple LLM calls with intermediate processing steps

bestPractices: Use hybrid search combining semantic and keyword matching for better retrieval accuracy
Implement caching for deterministic prompts to reduce latency and costs
Track key metrics like latency, token usage, and user satisfaction for continuous improvement
antiPatterns: Using fixed-size chunking without considering document structure, which breaks context
Skipping evaluation and monitoring, making it impossible to detect quality degradation
Not implementing fallback strategies when primary LLM providers experience outages

faq.question: What is the difference between RAG and fine-tuning?
faq.answer: RAG retrieves relevant documents at query time and provides them as context, allowing the model to access up-to-date information without retraining. Fine-tuning adjusts model weights on training data, which is better for learning style or format but cannot add new knowledge after training.

faq.question: How do I choose between different agent architectures?
faq.answer: Use ReAct for interactive multi-step reasoning with tool use. Use function calling for structured API integrations. Use Plan-and-Execute for complex tasks requiring upfront planning. Use multi-agent systems when specialized expertise is needed for different subtasks.

faq.question: What vector database should I use for production?
faq.answer: For managed service with high scale, use Pinecone. For self-hosted with GraphQL, use Weaviate. For existing PostgreSQL infrastructure, use pgvector. For prototyping, use ChromaDB for simplicity.

faq.question: How can I reduce LLM API costs?
faq.answer: Implement semantic caching for similar queries, use smaller models for simple tasks, optimize prompt length, set appropriate temperature (0 for cacheable responses), and use fallback models when primary is rate-limited.

faq.question: What metrics should I track for LLM applications?
faq.answer: Track performance metrics (latency, tokens per second), quality metrics (user satisfaction, task completion rate), cost metrics (cost per request, cache hit rate), and reliability metrics (error rate, timeout rate).

faq.question: How do I handle hallucinations in RAG systems?
faq.answer: Instruct the model to say 'I don't know' when context is insufficient, use groundedness scoring in evaluation, implement retrieval quality checks, and consider adding a verification step where the model cites sources for claims.
